headline: Nvidia–Coherent deal, Paramount+ to be combined with HBO Max
summary: Market Domination host Josh Lipton and Yahoo Finance Senior Reporter Brooke DiPalma take a look at some of Monday's trending tickers and stories. HBO Max and Paramount+ will be combining into a single streaming platform. This comes after Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) accepted Paramount's (PSKY) acquisition bid. Elevance (ELV) stock is dropping after the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services planned sanctions on the company. Nvidia (NVDA) and Coherent (COHR) announced a strategic partnership on developing optics technology. To watch more expert insights and analysis on the latest market action, check out more Market Domination.

headline: Paramount CEO Ellison unveils sweeping plan for Warner Bros
summary: STORY: Paramount Skydance CEO David Ellison unveiled his plans for Warner Bros Discovery on Monday, after clinching a deal to acquire the media giant.On a call with analysts, Ellison said the combined company will have a net debt load of about $79 billion.He ruled out any plans to divest or spinoff Warner Bros' cable TV networks, which include CNN and HBO.Instead, the merger will unite them with Paramount TV assets CBS, MTV, Comedy Central and BET.As for the two companies' streaming services, which include Paramount+ and HBO Max, Ellison said those will be folded into a single streaming platform.:: NetflixHe noted that Paramount and Warner Bros already serve more than 200 million streaming customers, giving them the scale and firepower to better compete with industry leader Netflix.Paramount signed the $110 billion deal for Warner Bros on Friday after a months-long battle with rival bidder Netflix, which declined to raise its offer.Paramount also paid the $2.8 billion termination fee that Warner Bros owed Netflix.The Paramount-Warner deal is expected to close in the third quarter of this year.Ellison's father, billionaire Larry Ellison, has deep ties to the Trump administration, a factor some analysts said could help it secure more favorable regulatory treatment.

raw.headline: Paramount CEO Ellison unveils sweeping plan for Warner Bros
raw.summary: STORY: Paramount Skydance CEO David Ellison unveiled his plans for Warner Bros Discovery on Monday, after clinching a deal to acquire the media giant.On a call with analysts, Ellison said the combined company will have a net debt load of about $79 billion.He ruled out any plans to divest or spinoff Warner Bros' cable TV networks, which include CNN and HBO.Instead, the merger will unite them with Paramount TV assets CBS, MTV, Comedy Central and BET.As for the two companies' streaming services, which include Paramount+ and HBO Max, Ellison said those will be folded into a single streaming platform.:: NetflixHe noted that Paramount and Warner Bros already serve more than 200 million streaming customers, giving them the scale and firepower to better compete with industry leader Netflix.Paramount signed the $110 billion deal for Warner Bros on Friday after a months-long battle with rival bidder Netflix, which declined to raise its offer.Paramount also paid the $2.8 billion termination fee that Warner Bros owed Netflix.The Paramount-Warner deal is expected to close in the third quarter of this year.Ellison's father, billionaire Larry Ellison, has deep ties to the Trump administration, a factor some analysts said could help it secure more favorable regulatory treatment.

headline: Paramount Skydance Warner Bros Discovery Deal Reshapes Streaming Competition Risks
summary: Paramount Skydance has agreed to acquire Warner Bros. Discovery (NasdaqGS:WBD) in a blockbuster media deal after a competitive bidding war. The transaction follows the termination of Warner Bros. Discovery's earlier merger agreement with Netflix, whose proposal was judged less attractive. The combined group will bring together major film and TV studios, streaming platforms, and extensive content libraries, drawing close regulatory and political scrutiny. Warner Bros. Discovery, home to HBO,...

headline: Markets react to geopolitical risks, BYD teases 'disruptive tech'
summary: Yahoo Finance's John Hyland tracks today's top moving stocks and biggest market stories in this Market Minute, including how equities (^DJI, ^IXIC, ^GSPC), oil prices (CL=F, BZ=F), and US bond yields (^TYX, ^TNX, ^FVX) are reacting to rising geopolitical risk following US airstrikes on Iran; BYD (1211.HK, BYDDY) shares are gaining after hinting at a new "disruptive" technology; and Paramount Skydance (PSKY) stock takes a dip. Stay up to date on the latest market action, minute-by-minute, with Yahoo Finance's Market Minute.

headline: Paramount debt to hit $79 billion after Warner Bros deal, no plan to sell cable assets
summary: The Warner Bros-Paramount Skydance merger will create a combined entity that would have a net debt of about $79 billion, Paramount said on Monday, as it ruled out any plan to ‌divest or spinoff the cable assets.  This was disclosed by Paramount CEO David Ellison in a call with analysts ‌after signing the $110 billion, or $31-per-share, deal for Warner Bros early on Friday, after Netflix declined to raise its offer.  The two companies will fold their ​streaming services into a single platform, Ellison said, giving Paramount the scale and firepower needed to compete more effectively in a market dominated by Netflix.

raw.headline: Paramount debt to hit $79 billion after Warner Bros deal, no plan to sell cable assets
raw.summary: The Warner Bros-Paramount Skydance merger will create a combined entity that would have a net debt of about $79 billion, Paramount said on Monday, as it ruled out any plan to ‌divest or spinoff the cable assets.  This was disclosed by Paramount CEO David Ellison in a call with analysts ‌after signing the $110 billion, or $31-per-share, deal for Warner Bros early on Friday, after Netflix declined to raise its offer.  The two companies will fold their ​streaming services into a single platform, Ellison said, giving Paramount the scale and firepower needed to compete more effectively in a market dominated by Netflix.

headline: War, Credit Cracks & the AI Divide: Wall Street Closes a Brutal Week as the Middle East Ignites
summary: Friday's session was already under pressure from private credit contagion and AI sector fatigue, then the weekend delivered an entirely different kind of shockwave. The US and Israel launched military strikes on Iran, ayatollah Khamenei was killed, and OPEC+ surprised markets with a larger-than-expected production increase. This morning, every investor on the planet is recalibrating.
